replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Daniel Bradford ok mate - well whatever strategy works for you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Daniel Bradford I was just thinking a bit more about this - most patents never make it as far as a product. The number is huge something like 95%. This isn't a conspiracy thing it's just the way it is because people are innately conservative about stuff. I can more or less guarantee that people won't stumble across your patent, recognise the value of it and come to you wanting to produce it. There are lots of reason for that and the least likely is conspiracy - and if you don't believe me - give it 20 years and you will find no one has been knocking at your door. There is only one person right now who genuinely believes in your invention and that is you. Nobody is going to be a better champion of this than you. If you don't shout about it - well - the chances are nothing is going to happen with it. I hate to say this - i really do - but that is what will happen - in all likelihood.

I would try polystyrene beads, I think there are specific polystyrene beads that are produced specifically to be nonmagnetic.

author: @joeglory425
text: Impressive discovery! Your use of a control is very helpful in that it helps to put the results into perspective.  For example if the results were due to galvanic effects you would see it in the control as well as the experimental, but there is a very definite difference between the two!

author: @talentguydotcom
text: Is there some configuration that you can think of to tap into the casimir effect???

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i guess if you made it small enough

replies.author: @talentguydotcom
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith If you put the ink between a solenoid (  https://www.google.com/search?q=solenoid+flux&espv=2&biw=1366&bih=667&source=lnms&tbm=isch&sa=X&ei=i9RCVKefLoj1iQLnn4DwBA&ved=0CAcQ_AUoAg#facrc=_&imgdii=_&imgrc=XnU6c6wDBGzNaM%253A%3BSGjv9074d8yfqM%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252Fwhs.wsd.wednet.edu%252Ffaculty%252Fbusse%252Fmathhomepage%252Fbusseclasses%252Fradiationphysics%252Flecturenotes%252Fchapter4%252Fgraphics%252Fsolenoid2.gif%3Bhttp%253A%252F%252Fwhs.wsd.wednet.edu%252Ffaculty%252Fbusse%252Fmathhomepage%252Fbusseclasses%252Fradiationphysics%252Flecturenotes%252Fchapter4%252Fchapter4.html%3B499%3B378 ) field so that all of the flux line draw infinitely thin parallel line patterns, and then connect those with the copper particles you might be able to make infinitely close capacitor boundaries.

After the plastic is coated, maybe you can curl it and see if the casimir effect causes a mechanical oscillation.

i guess

author: @bigwoofbouncer
text: That's really interesting. I wonder if a linear pattern like this is acting as multiple cells or somehow slowing down the run off and increasing the interaction time. I'd be curious to see what other patterns, such as diamonds might do and whether a magnetic field is required when producing them or if simply printing them onto the surface (perhaps building up several layers) would yield similar results. Keep up the great work... Fascinating!

replies.author: @clixbits
replies.text: Snap!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It is more likely to be the orientation helping with conduction - or at least that's what i think. I don't think there is anything particularly 'fancy' going on here and I don't think 'fancy' patterns will help much - the field just lets the nanoparticles line up to create a direct route to the current collectors reducing the internal resistance and there by giving an improved result. I am sure it could be printed if you could get that much control over the lining up - but that would be far more challenging to do than laying it on a bed and drying it
